focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

By noon Gideon had dressed Patent Row for a wedding between commerce and obedience.

The old cooperage wall had been skinned in fresh lime and damp plaster, smooth as a rich man's lie. Verdancy House bunting drooped from the scaffold in strips of pale green silk that looked refined from the middle of the street and fungus-sick when a person got close enough to see the water marks. Two hired boys in white gloves stood guard over trays of handbills and paper crowns. A string quartet, paid well enough to hate itself, kept sawing out something solemn while the whole block sweated under them. Even the gutters had been sluiced. They still smelled of eel rot, brewer's mash, and the sweet dead breath of warm pond water, but now they smelled of those things in public clothes.

Silas stood on the scaffold plank with a mahlstick under one arm and his brush roll at his hip, staring at the wall that was supposed to save him.

Celadon Crown had sounded expensive the first time Silas said it across Gideon's desk and watched the man smile like a till springing open. It sounded more expensive now, printed in soft green on the handbills, set in looping Roman letters above the promise of regular color, regular heat, regular supply. It was the sort of name that knew which fork to use. It belonged in Gilt Crescent under cut glass and folded napkins. It did not belong in a cellar where a woman needed to know if her soup would scorch or if the lamp by the stairs would stay cool enough for a child not to blister two fingers on the cage. Inez had shown him too many kitchens, lamp cages, and wet steps for the word to sit clean in his mouth anymore. Silas knew that now. He knew too much now. None of it had stopped him from wearing his best coat and turning up early with his brushes c...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...on the page because the air had swelled the fibers. His signature looked too decorative for the occasion, all the old flourish still clinging to the S of his name like vanity refusing to die when called.

He signed anyway.

Gideon slid the envelope across at once, efficient as a banker closing a drawer. He rose, replaced his glove, and collected the contract with a care that made it plain he already considered the thing filed inside the future.

"You have done wisely," he said.

"I've done expensively," Silas said.

"Wisdom often presents that way at first."

Junie opened the door for him with a politeness so bare it functioned as insult. Gideon stepped back into the damp lane, his shoes avoiding the worst of the puddled grit without seeming to look down. Then he was gone, a dark clean stroke moving up toward the better-lit streets.

Silas stayed at the table with the envelope in his hand. The paper was thick, warm from Gideon's pocket. Junie did not ask to see inside it. She knew the weight of money without counting by touch. Otis slept on, breathing milk and chalk and child sweat into the cooling room.

"Well," Junie said at last.

Silas thumbed the flap open and looked at the notes. Real notes. Enough to turn several kinds of disaster slightly sideways.

"Yeah," he said.

That was all. Nothing noble fit in the kitchen after a choice like that. The stove ticked. The basin water slowly lost its last heat. Outside, somebody on the landing laughed and said bridelight, making it sound like nothing more than a pretty thing to have in the mouth.

Silas sat with Gideon's money in his hand and knew at least this much clearly: whatever the moss answered to next, his need would be in the word, and by morning need would send him back toward the wards that knew more than he did.
